runs.t: Imagine you need to store 30 student marks. Without arrays you would need 30 separate variables —
. Arrays are a Grade 11 Term 1 topic.
holds an ordered list of values. Think of it like a train — each carriage (box) holds one value and has a number (index). The index tells you where to find the value.

blocks.label: Examples

blocks.text: Arrays of Unknown Length — use a constant

runs.t: You don't always know in advance exactly how many items you will store. A common trick is to declare a
for the maximum size, then use it as the upper index. If the size needs to change later, you only edit one line.

blocks.title: Declare the constant BEFORE the array

runs.t: the array declaration (it can go just above
in the interface section), otherwise Delphi won't know what

blocks.label: Delphi
blocks.text:
const
  Max = 100;                          // maximum possible learners
var
  arrNames : array [1..Max] of String;
  iCount   : Integer;                  // how many are ACTUALLY used

runs.t: ) for how many elements are really in use, and loop
when declared — you set (and can later change) its length while the program runs with
. This is perfect when the number of items is only known at runtime (e.g. after reading a file).

rows.t: Sets (or changes) the array to hold
Returns the current number of elements
First valid index (always 0 for dynamic arrays)

blocks.label: Delphi — dynamic array
blocks.text:
var
  arrNum : array of Integer;   // note: NO index range given
  i : Integer;
begin
  SetLength(arrNum, 5);          // now holds 5 ints, index 0..4

  for i := Low(arrNum) to High(arrNum) do
    arrNum[i] := i * 2;           // 0, 2, 4, 6, 8

  SetLength(arrNum, 8);          // grow to 8 — existing values are kept
end;
Method 2: Constant array (known at compile time)
Method 3: User input with a loop

blocks.label: Delphi
blocks.text:
for i := 1 to 30 do
  arrNames[i] := InputBox('Name', 'Enter name:', '');

runs.t: Text files allow your Delphi program to save and load data that persists between runs. Learn the four operations: reading, writing, appending, and checking existence.

is a file that stores plain, readable characters with no special formatting — just letters, numbers and line breaks, exactly as you would type them into Notepad. There are no fonts, colours, or hidden layout codes like you would find in a Word document; it is simply text, one line after another.

Think of a text file as a plain notepad. You can open it, read what is written, add a new line at the bottom, or tear out the page and start fresh. There is nothing fancy inside — just the words you wrote.
The variables in your program only live in memory (RAM) while the program is running. The moment you close the program, everything in those variables is gone. A file gives your data
— it is saved onto the disk so that it survives after the program closes and is still there the next time you run it.
a marks program that lets a teacher capture results is useless if every mark vanishes when she closes it. By writing the marks to a text file, the data is safely stored and can be read back tomorrow.
When working with files in Delphi there are two "names" to keep straight:
– the real name of the file as it exists on the disk, for example
. This is what you would see in File Explorer.
. Your code talks to this variable, not directly to the disk.
command is what links the two together — it tells Delphi "whenever I use the logical variable
, I really mean the physical file
is the actual postbox standing in the street with a real street address. The
you stick on your desk that says "this drawer represents that postbox".
is the act of writing the street address onto your label so the two are connected.
Before looking at the table, here is each command explained in plain words:
– links your file variable to the real file on disk. Always the very first step.
– opens an existing file so you can
from it, starting at the very beginning.
– creates a brand-new empty file for writing. If the file already exists, its old contents are wiped out, so use it with care.
– opens an existing file and moves to the very end, so new lines are
without destroying what is already there.
– reads one whole line from the file into the variable
, then moves on to the next line.
into the file as one line.
– saves and properly closes the file. Like shutting and locking the postbox so nothing is lost.
once you have read the last line, so your loop knows when to stop.

rows.t: Links the file variable F to a file on disk
Reads one line from file into variable S
Writes variable S as one line to the file
Returns True when the end of the file is reached
Returns True if the file exists on disk

blocks.label: Delphi — read all lines
Delphi — create / overwrite
Delphi — add to end
Delphi — read Name,Mark pairs

runs.t: means the file remains locked and data may not be saved properly.
User-defined procedures and functions let you break code into reusable, named blocks. This makes programs easier to read, maintain and debug.
is a named block of code that performs a specific task. Instead of writing the same instructions over and over inside your event handlers, you write them
, give that block a name, and then simply call that name whenever you need the task done. In Delphi we build methods in two forms:

Think of a method like a recipe card in a cookbook. The recipe is written down once. Whenever you want that dish, you don't rewrite the steps from scratch — you just fetch the card and follow it. A method works exactly the same way: write the steps once, then "fetch" them by name as often as you like.
is a named block of code that
send a value back to where it was called. It simply carries out its instructions — showing a message, clearing some boxes, drawing on a canvas — and then control returns to the program.
A procedure is like asking someone to "switch off the lights". They go and do the job. You don't expect them to hand you anything back — you just expect the task to be done.
is a named block of code that does a task
and then returns exactly one value
back to where it was called. Inside the function you store the answer in the special variable
, and that answer is handed back so you can use it — store it, display it, or calculate further with it.
A function is like asking someone "How much is the bread?" They go, check, and come
you can use. A function always brings something back; a procedure does not have to.

blocks.text: Why Do We Break Code Into Methods?

runs.t: When you are starting out it can feel like extra work to split your program into little named blocks. But experienced programmers do it for very good reasons:
– if the same instructions appear in three places and you copy-paste them, you now have to fix bugs in three places. Put them in one method and you fix it once.
tells the reader exactly what is happening, far better than ten lines of arithmetic.
– you can test one small method on its own instead of hunting through a giant block of code.
– once written, a method can be called from many different buttons and events.
. If a method is trying to do several things at once, that is usually a sign it should be split into smaller methods.
is a placeholder name listed in the method's declaration — it stands for a value the method will receive. An
is the actual value you pass
when you call the method. In short: parameters live in the declaration; arguments are the real values you supply at the call.

Think of a parameter as a labelled jar called "sName" sitting empty on the shelf when the recipe is written. The argument is the actual "Ms Coetzee" you scoop into that jar when you finally cook. The jar's label is the parameter; what you put in it is the argument.

of the argument. The method can change that copy freely inside itself, but the original variable back in the calling code stays untouched. This is the default and safest kind of parameter, because the caller never has to worry about its variables being changed unexpectedly.
A value parameter is like giving someone a
of a document. They can scribble all over their copy, but your original is safe at home.

rows.t: Yes — always returns exactly one value

blocks.label: Delphi — declare and call
Delphi
Delphi

runs.t: To bring it all together, here are the practical benefits you gain every time you reach for a method instead of writing one long block of code:
Break large programs into manageable pieces
Reuse code from multiple event handlers — no duplication
Easier to find and fix bugs
Easier to update — change in one place, works everywhere
Each method should perform only one task

and use SQL statements to do the same things — faster and more powerful.

blocks.label: Delphi — examples

runs.t: Think of records as pages in a book. These methods let you turn pages:

rows.t: Jump to the very first record
Returns True when you have gone past the last record (End Of File). Use in a WHILE loop to process all records.

blocks.label: Delphi — loop through ALL records
blocks.text:
dm.tblStudents.First;                        // start at record 1
while not dm.tblStudents.Eof do           // keep going until past last
begin
  memOut.Lines.Add(dm.tblStudents['Name']); // do something with this record
  dm.tblStudents.Next;                       // move to next record
end;

runs.t: inside the loop, the program will be stuck on the first record forever — an infinite loop that will crash Delphi.
To find a specific record, loop through all records and check each one:

blocks.label: Delphi — find a student by surname

runs.t: Adding a new record is a 3-step process: prepare → fill in values → save.

blocks.label: Delphi
Delphi
blocks.text:
// First navigate to the record you want to change, then:
dm.tblStudents.Edit;                      // Step 1: put the record into edit mode
dm.tblStudents['Grade'] := 12;            // Step 2: change the value
dm.tblStudents.Post;                      // Step 3: save the change

blocks.label: Delphi
blocks.text:
// Navigate to the record first, then:
dm.tblStudents.Delete;   // removes the current record permanently

runs.t: There is no undo. Always confirm with the user before deleting:
if MessageDlg('Delete this record?', mtConfirmation, mbYesNo, 0) = mrYes then dm.tblStudents.Delete;

blocks.label: Delphi
blocks.text:
dm.tblStudents.Sort := 'Surname ASC';         // A to Z by surname
dm.tblStudents.Sort := 'Mark DESC';           // highest mark first
dm.tblStudents.Sort := 'Grade ASC, Surname ASC'; // by grade, then surname

A deeper look at what is inside the system unit — the motherboard, its components, buses, expansion cards, and how data flows between parts.
The motherboard is a large printed circuit board that connects and provides power to all hardware components. It is the central hub of the computer.

rows.t: Basic Input/Output System — first instructions when PC starts. Stored on ROM. Performs POST.
Connects the CPU to the motherboard
Add expansion cards (GPU, sound card, NIC)
Supplies power from PSU to all components
Volatile short-term memory for active programs
Read-Only Memory — stores BIOS firmware permanently

runs.t: Locates the bootloader and hands control to the OS
is a set of wires that transfers data between components.

rows.t: Transfers data and instructions between components
Carries the physical memory address of data
CPU sends signals to coordinate actions

runs.t: Data is loaded from slow storage into fast RAM, then the CPU fetches from RAM for processing.
Graphics data is moved from RAM to VRAM so the GPU can render images without burdening the CPU.
Cache is a very small, very fast memory located close to (or inside) the CPU. It stores frequently used instructions so the CPU does not have to wait for slower RAM.
— individual components (RAM, CPU, GPU) can be upgraded or replaced without replacing the whole computer.
Modern computers can execute multiple tasks simultaneously using multitasking, multithreading and multiprocessing. This page also covers virtual memory, compilers and interpreters.

is a method the operating system and CPU use to handle more than one job at a time so the computer feels fast and responsive. In the early days a computer could only do one thing from start to finish before moving on. Today we expect to type an email, listen to music and download a file all at once — and these techniques are what make that possible.

A CPU is incredibly fast, but a human is slow. While you pause to think about your next sentence, the CPU is sitting idle for millions of cycles. Processing techniques let the computer fill those gaps with other work, so no time is wasted and every program feels like it is running "at the same time".

blocks.title: THINK OF A CHEF IN A KITCHEN

runs.t: Imagine one chef preparing several dishes.
is the chef stirring one pot, then quickly turning to chop onions, then back to the pot — one pair of hands switching between jobs so fast it looks simultaneous.
is one big recipe (say a curry) broken into steps that move along together — the sauce simmers while the rice cooks, all part of the same dish.
is hiring several chefs (CPU cores), each cooking a whole dish at the same moment in real parallel.

rows.t: OS rapidly switches between multiple programs, giving the illusion of simultaneous execution
Listening to music while browsing the web
A single program is divided into threads that run concurrently
Browser: separate threads for UI, downloads, rendering, scripts
Multiple CPU cores execute tasks truly in parallel
A quad-core CPU running 4 tasks simultaneously

runs.t: – running several programs at once by switching rapidly between them. Only one task truly runs at any instant, but the switching is so quick it feels simultaneous.
– splitting a single program into smaller "threads" that progress together, so one slow part (like a download) does not freeze the rest of the program.
– using two or more CPU cores to genuinely execute different tasks at the same time. This is real parallelism, not an illusion.
The key difference to remember is that multitasking and multithreading
one processor by taking turns, while multiprocessing uses
processors to do work truly at the same moment.

is storage space on the hard drive that the operating system uses as if it were extra RAM. When the real RAM fills up, the OS borrows a slice of the much larger (but much slower) hard drive to keep programs running instead of crashing.

RAM is expensive and limited. Without virtual memory, the moment your RAM filled up the computer would simply refuse to open the next program. Virtual memory acts as a safety valve so you can keep working even when memory is tight.

blocks.title: A DESK AND A FILING CABINET

runs.t: as the top of your desk — small, but everything on it is right in front of you and instantly reachable. The

is a big filing cabinet across the room — it holds far more, but you must get up and fetch things from it. When your desk overflows, you move papers you are not using right now into the cabinet (virtual memory) and fetch them back when you need them. It works, but every trip to the cabinet wastes time.
When RAM is full, the OS uses a section of the hard drive as temporary RAM — called
Programs not currently in use are swapped from RAM to virtual memory
When needed again, they are swapped back into RAM
Allows more programs to run than physical RAM would normally allow
Much slower than real RAM — heavy use causes

is what happens when the computer spends more time swapping data back and forth between RAM and virtual memory than it spends doing actual useful work. The hard drive light stays on constantly, the mouse stutters, and everything slows to a crawl. In our desk analogy, thrashing is when your desk is so small that you are constantly running to the filing cabinet and back, never getting anything done.
(binary 1s and 0s), but humans write in readable programming languages. A
is needed to bridge that gap. There are two main kinds, and the difference is all about
source code into machine code in one go, before the program ever runs, producing a standalone executable file.
– a translator that reads and runs the code
, translating each line just as it is about to be executed.
Think of translating a book. A
is like a translator who sits down and translates the whole book into a finished printed copy — slow to prepare, but afterwards anyone can read it instantly. An

is like a live human interpreter standing next to a speaker, translating each sentence out loud as it is spoken — you can start immediately, but it is slower overall because translation happens during the conversation.

rows.t: Translates entire source code to machine code at once
Reads and executes one line at a time
Slower to open program, faster once running
Faster to open, slower while running
Runs until error is hit, then crashes

runs.t: is the technique of using software to create a "virtual" computer — called a
— that runs inside your real computer as if it were a separate physical machine with its own operating system.

One powerful computer can be split into several virtual machines, so a single server can do the work of many. This saves money, electricity and space, and lets you safely test risky software in a sealed-off environment without endangering your real system.

Imagine one large building (your physical computer). Virtualisation divides it into several self-contained flats (virtual machines). Each flat has its own front door, kitchen and tenants (its own operating system and programs), and what happens in one flat does not affect the others — yet they all share the same building, plumbing and electricity (the real hardware).
Running Windows on a Mac, or a single cloud server hosting dozens of separate websites, each in its own virtual machine.

rows.t: Close to machine code; fast and efficient; hard to read
English-like; easier to write and understand; compiled or interpreted
Crashes while running (e.g. divide by zero)
Calculation result too large for the data type

runs.t: — instead of papers stuffed into folders, your data is stored in neat tables with rows and columns, so you can find, sort and filter it in seconds. A
(Database Management System) is the software that manages it all.

rows.t: An organised collection of data stored in tables
Rows and columns storing one type of entity (e.g. Students)
One complete row — all data about one item
One column — a specific attribute (e.g. Surname, Age)
Unique field that identifies each record (e.g. StudentID)
A field that links to the primary key of another table
Database Management System — software to create, manage and query databases

blocks.text: What a Database Table Looks Like

runs.t: A table in a database looks exactly like a spreadsheet grid. The table has a name, column headers (fields), and rows of data (records).
Keys are what make databases powerful — they link tables together so you do not repeat data.

rows.t: Uniquely identifies each record. Cannot be NULL. Cannot repeat.
A field in one table that refers to the PK of another table.
ClassID in tblStudents → ClassID in tblClasses
Two or more fields combined to form a unique identifier.

runs.t: An ERD shows the tables in a database and how they are related. The line between tables shows the type of relationship (one-to-many, etc.).
Each field in a table has a data type that controls what kind of data can be stored.

rows.t: Dates and/or times stored as numeric values internally
Has paid school fees, is a prefect
Automatically assigns the next unique integer — ideal for PK
Monetary values with fixed decimal precision
Small databases, school/office, learning, Grade 11–12 IT practicals
One record in Table A links to exactly one record in Table B
Student ↔ Locker (each student has one locker)
One record in Table A links to many records in Table B
Class → Students (one class has many students)
Many records in A link to many records in B (needs a linking table)
Students ↔ Subjects (students take many subjects; subjects have many students)

runs.t: Data quality means the data is
. Poor quality data leads to wrong decisions.

rows.t: Data is correct and free from errors
A learner's mark recorded as 78, not 87 (transposition error)
Data conforms to defined rules and constraints
Data is up to date — not outdated
A learner's address updated after they move house
All required data is present — no blank fields where required
Every learner record has a valid ID number and grade
Only data that is needed is stored
A school database does not need to store a learner's favourite colour

runs.t: ensures data is accurate, consistent and reliable over its entire lifetime.
— protection from hardware failure using RAID, backups, and UPS.
— data follows rules: input validation, referential integrity, access control.
— a FK value must exist as a PK in the related table (no orphan records).
Validation checks that data entered is
reasonable and in the correct format
— it does not check if data is true, just if it is acceptable.

rows.t: Data matches required pattern or structure
Email must contain @ and a dot —
Value falls within acceptable minimum and maximum
Test mark must be between 0 and 100
Data entered is the correct data type
Age field must be a number, not text
The field must not be blank (required)
Surname cannot be empty — every learner must have a surname
A calculated digit is appended and re-calculated to verify correctness
SA ID number: last digit is a check digit
Value must match an item in a predefined list
Province field: only one of the 9 SA province names allowed
Two or more fields must be logically consistent
If Grade = 10, Year must be after 2005 (15-year-old)
Single user, stored locally on one computer
MS Access for a school IT project
Multi-user, stored on a server, accessed over a network
School administration system accessed by teachers and admin
A national learner database accessed from all provinces

runs.t: — designs structure, models relationships, plans migrations
— analyses large datasets to find patterns and insights
— manages database projects, budgets and teams
Building on Grade 10 networks — this page covers topologies, network devices in depth, protocols, VoIP, VPN and acceptable use.

is the layout or "shape" of a network — it describes how the computers and devices are physically or logically arranged and connected to one another. The topology you choose affects how easy the network is to set up, how it copes with a fault, and how easy it is to add new devices later.
every device connects to one central point — usually a switch. Nothing connects directly to anything else; all traffic passes through the middle.

Picture a bicycle wheel: the hub in the centre is the switch, and each spoke is a cable running out to one computer. If a single spoke breaks the rest of the wheel is fine — but if the hub itself fails, the whole wheel falls apart. That is exactly why a star network keeps working when one PC fails, yet goes completely down if the central switch fails.

rows.t: All devices connect to a central switch
One failure doesn't affect others; easy to add devices
If switch fails, entire network fails
Equal opportunity to transmit; no collision
One break can down the whole network
School lab PC connected to server

runs.t: allows phone/video calls using the Internet instead of traditional phone lines.

Your voice is broken into tiny digital packets, sent across the internet just like any other data, and reassembled at the other end. Because it rides on your existing internet connection, calls to other VoIP users are usually free no matter how far away they are — which makes it far cheaper than a traditional long-distance phone call.
Examples: WhatsApp calls, Skype, Discord, Zoom
Requires: stable internet, low latency, sufficient bandwidth
creates an encrypted "tunnel" over the Internet.
Allows remote users to access a private network securely
Hides IP address and encrypts all data in transit
Used by companies for remote workers
Rules governing how ICT resources may be used in an organisation. Key rules:
Mobile devices have transformed communication and computing. This page covers smartphones, tablets, wireless technologies, and location-based services.
is any portable, wireless technology that lets you communicate, compute and access information while on the move, without being tied to a desk or a cable. The key idea is
: the device travels with you, connects wirelessly, and runs off its own battery.

In South Africa mobile technology is especially important. Many people first reach the internet not through a desktop PC but through a smartphone, because data on a phone is often cheaper and more accessible than a fixed-line connection at home. This makes the smartphone the main computer in millions of households.

– a pocket-sized computer that combines a phone, camera, GPS, web browser and thousands of apps. It connects through Wi-Fi and the cellular network and is by far the most common mobile device in South Africa.
– a larger touchscreen device, ideal for reading, watching media and light productivity. The bigger screen makes it more comfortable than a phone, but it is less pocket-friendly.
– devices worn on the body, such as smartwatches and fitness bands. They track health data like heart rate and steps, and show notifications from your phone.
uses signals from satellites to work out exactly where a device is on Earth, which is what powers navigation apps.
– a short-range wireless technology (about 10m) used to connect devices to each other, such as wireless earphones, keyboards or a car's hands-free system.
– a wireless technology that connects devices to the internet through a router or hotspot over a medium range, usually inside a home, school or office.
gave us reliable mobile browsing and video;
is far faster and more responsive, enabling things like real-time video and large numbers of connected smart devices.

blocks.text: Advantages and Limitations of Mobile Technology

runs.t: – you can work, bank, learn and communicate from anywhere; devices are convenient and always with you; and a single phone replaces many separate gadgets (camera, torch, calculator, GPS).

– small screens and keyboards are harder to work on for long periods; battery life is limited; mobile data can be expensive in South Africa; and the constant connection raises privacy, security and "always-on" distraction concerns.

rows.t: High-res touchscreen, app store, camera, Wi-Fi, cellular, GPS
Larger screen (7"+), touch input, iOS/Android, media and productivity apps
Fitness tracking, heart rate, notifications, GPS
E-ink display, long battery, sunlight readable, primarily for books

runs.t: Software that uses your device's location (via GPS, Wi-Fi, cellular) to provide customised services.
Weather apps showing your local forecast
Food delivery apps delivering to your address
— ISP prioritises certain traffic types (e.g. video gets priority over downloads)
— ISP intentionally slows your speed after you exceed a data cap
Safeguarding a computer system requires understanding threats, knowing how malware works, and applying the right remedies.
Sharing sensitive information with wrong people
Theft of devices or storage media
Power surges or outages damaging hardware

rows.t: Attaches to files; replicates when file is run; causes damage
Spreads across networks without user action
Disguised as useful software; gives attacker back-door access
Hides in OS; gives remote attacker control; avoids detection
Encrypts data; demands payment to restore access
Secretly monitors activity and steals data
Fake official-looking emails to steal credentials
Forges sender addresses to impersonate someone
Malicious SQL commands entered via web forms
Detects, removes and prevents malware infections
Filters all incoming and outgoing network traffic
8+ chars; mix of uppercase, lowercase, numbers, symbols; unique per site
Users only access files they are authorised for
Scrambles data so it cannot be read if intercepted
Keeps computer running during power failure
Requires additional verification beyond just a password
Only files changed since last backup (any type)
Files changed since last full backup

runs.t: — external HDD at same location (fast, but vulnerable to fire/theft)
— physical device at another location (protects against local disaster)
stored off-site or in the cloud.
Mobile and wireless communication forms — blogs, podcasts, VoIP, instant messaging, video conferencing and webinars.

(electronic communication) is any way of sending and receiving information using electronic devices and the internet, instead of paper or face-to-face talking. It covers everything from a short WhatsApp message to a live video meeting with people on different continents.

is a regularly updated online journal where one person or organisation publishes articles ("posts"), with the newest post at the top. It is mostly one-way: the author writes, readers read. Think of it as a public diary anyone can visit.
– the same idea as blogging but with
posts, like on X (Twitter). It is designed for quick, frequent updates shared with followers rather than long articles.
text message sent over the cellular network. Its great strength is that it needs
, which is why banks still use it for one-time PINs.
– real-time text and media chat over the internet, such as WhatsApp. Messages arrive instantly and you can chat one-on-one or in groups.
carries your voice (and video) over the internet instead of the old telephone network, so calls between VoIP users are usually free.
– a live meeting where several people see and hear each other on video at the same time, such as on Zoom or Teams. Because you can see faces, it captures nonverbal cues that a phone call cannot.
– an audio series you can download and listen to offline, like a radio show on demand. Listeners subscribe and new episodes arrive automatically.
A blog is like a public diary, microblogging is like shouting a quick headline across a crowd, and a podcast is like your own radio station that listeners can tune into whenever they choose.

rows.t: Online journal; one-way content; new posts appear at top
Very short posts (text/image/video) shared with followers
140-char text via mobile network; no internet needed
Free real-time text/media over internet; group chats
Audio-only series; downloadable for offline listening
Voice/video calls over internet; free to other VoIP users
Live multi-person video; includes nonverbal communication
Online seminar with interactive features (polls, Q&A)

blocks.text: Advantages and Disadvantages of Key Forms

rows.t: Not permanently backed up by default
Can be a distraction; creates pressure to respond
Can be recorded for later viewing
All participants must be available simultaneously

runs.t: Because e-communications travel across networks that strangers also use, keeping your messages private and your accounts safe is essential. A few key ideas help here:
encrypts the link between your browser and a website;
scrambles a message so that only the sender and the intended receiver can read it — not even the service in the middle; and
adds a second proof of identity (like a code on your phone) on top of your password, so a stolen password alone is not enough to break in.
Use HTTPS for all web communication
End-to-end encryption (only sender and receiver can read)
Avoid public Wi-Fi for sensitive communications
The evolution of the web, Big Data concepts, streaming vs downloading, multimedia formats and compression technologies.

rows.t: Static HTML pages; users can only read, not interact. (1990s)
Interactive content; user-generated content; social media; blogs; wikis. (2000s–present)

runs.t: Big Data refers to extremely large and complex datasets that traditional software cannot process effectively.

rows.t: Massive amounts of data generated every second
Speed at which data is created and must be processed
Data comes in many forms: text, images, video, sensor data, etc.

runs.t: Healthcare: predict patient risk and personalise treatment
Retail: recommend products based on browsing and purchase history
Banking: detect fraud in real time

rows.t: Plays in real time as data arrives

runs.t: Content streamed in real time as it happens — no pre-recording. Examples: live sport, news, gaming streams, webinars, online concerts.
: watch any content at any time — Netflix, Showmax, Disney+
: TV over the internet; allows pausing live TV, time-shifting, catch-up TV
Compression reduces file size to speed up transfer and save storage. The trade-off: higher compression = smaller file but lower quality.

rows.t: Music — removes sounds humans can barely hear
Photos — removes fine detail to reduce size
Online streaming — high quality, smaller file
DVDs and early digital TV (older standard)
Files and folders — full recovery on decompression

runs.t: : permanently removes data to get smaller files. Cannot fully recover original.
: compresses without losing data. Decompressed file is identical to original.
Types of websites, supporting web technologies, security services, and internet-related careers.

is any facility the internet provides that lets people find, share, communicate or transact online — from browsing websites to streaming video to online banking. The websites you visit are the most visible internet service, and they come in many types depending on their purpose.
Not all websites are built the same way. A
shows the same fixed pages to everyone, and only changes when the developer manually edits the HTML files. A
builds its pages on the spot, pulling fresh content out of a database every time you visit, so what you see can differ from what the next person sees.

blocks.title: A PRINTED POSTER vs A DIGITAL NOTICEBOARD

runs.t: is like a printed poster on a wall — it says exactly the same thing to everyone until someone takes it down and pins up a new one. A
is like a digital noticeboard wired to a computer — it can greet you by name, show your shopping cart, and update prices automatically because it fetches the latest information each time.

rows.t: Fixed HTML; only changes when developer edits
Generated from a database on demand
Online shops, social media, news sites

runs.t: is the set of rules browsers and web servers use to send web pages to each other.
is the same protocol with an added layer of security (the "S" stands for Secure) that encrypts the data so nobody can read it while it travels across the internet.

When you log in to your bank or type in your password, that information passes through many computers on its way across the internet. With plain HTTP, anyone who intercepts it can read it. HTTPS scrambles it into nonsense that only the destination can unlock.

blocks.title: A POSTCARD vs A LOCKED ENVELOPE

runs.t: is like writing your message on a
— every postal worker who handles it can read it along the way. Sending data over
is like sealing it inside a
that only the person you are writing to has the key to open. Always look for the padlock and "https://" before typing anything personal.
Requires more than one proof of identity. Examples: password + SMS code; password + fingerprint.
: much harder for attackers to access accounts
: requires additional device; SMS can be intercepted
A code sent to your device, valid for one login only. Prevents replay attacks even if code is intercepted later.
A hardware device or app that generates a new code every 30–60 seconds. More secure than SMS-based OTPs.

rows.t: Designs visual layout and user experience of websites (colours, fonts, layout)
Writes HTML, CSS and JavaScript to build and maintain websites
Creates logos, icons, animations, videos and interactive media
Manages website databases, security, backups and performance
Optimises websites to rank higher in search results
Monitors and protects systems from attacks

runs.t: The impact of technology on society — covering IoT, Big Data, the Fourth Industrial Revolution, cybercrime, and digital ethics.
A network of physical devices embedded with sensors and internet connectivity that collect and exchange data automatically.

rows.t: Soil sensors, weather monitoring for crop yields
Adaptive lighting, smart bins, traffic management

runs.t: Extremely large and complex datasets that traditional software cannot handle. Characteristics:
A distributed digital ledger where data is stored in linked, tamper-resistant "blocks".
Highly secure — changing one block breaks the chain
Used for: cryptocurrency (Bitcoin), smart contracts, supply chain tracking

rows.t: Stealing personal info to commit fraud
Stealing company secrets or customer databases
Encrypting company data and demanding ransom
Inserting malicious SQL into web forms to access databases

runs.t: is getting ideas, content, services or funding by asking a
(the "crowd"), usually online, rather than from traditional employees or suppliers. The internet makes it possible to reach huge numbers of contributors quickly.
taps into a wide range of skills and ideas cheaply and quickly.
contributions may be inaccurate, biased or unverified.
The trail of data you leave online.
: data collected without your knowledge (cookies, browsing history).
Implications: affects reputation, privacy, can be used by employers or cybercriminals.

runs.t: These topics are not explicitly required by CAPS but have appeared in NSC past papers (2015–2024). Knowing them can mean the difference between a pass and a distinction.
These Delphi functions, components and techniques are not listed in CAPS but appear regularly in exam questions or are needed to solve exam problems efficiently.

desc.t: Replaces all or first occurrence of a substring within a string.
s := StringReplace(s, 'old', 'new', [rfReplaceAll]);
A powerful list of strings — very useful for loading file lines into memory.
sl := TStringList.Create; sl.LoadFromFile('data.txt'); sl.Free;
Formats numbers/strings with precise control —
Format('%.2f', [rVal])
gives 2 decimal places. More powerful than FloatToStrF.
Generate random integers. Always call
Randomize;
first to seed the generator.
Random(100)
returns 0–99.
Let users browse for files at runtime.
if OpenDialog1.Execute then sFile := OpenDialog1.FileName;
Convert between TDate values and string representation. Used with TDateTimePicker.
sDate := DateToStr(dtpDate.Date);
Catch runtime errors gracefully — e.g. invalid StrToInt input.
try iNum := StrToInt(edtNum.Text); except ShowMessage('Invalid'); end;
Returns the number of lines in a Memo. Often used in loops to process all memo lines.
for i := 0 to Memo1.Lines.Count - 1 do
Case-insensitive string comparison — avoids problems when user types 'yes', 'YES' or 'Yes'.
if SameText(sInput, 'yes') then
Convert integers to hexadecimal strings and back. Sometimes tested in conjunction with number systems theory.
sHex := IntToHex(255, 4); // = '00FF'
Built-in boolean checks — cleaner than MOD 2.
if Odd(iNum) then // true if iNum is odd
Break
exits the loop immediately.
Continue
skips to the next iteration. Both appear in exam solutions — know the difference.

runs.t: These theory topics have appeared in NSC Theory papers but sit at the edge of or outside the formal CAPS scope. They typically appear in social implications or "current technology" questions.

desc.t: AI appears regularly in social implications questions — automation of jobs, AI decision-making, bias in AI, chatbots. Not deeply technical — understand the concept and its social effects.
A distributed digital ledger of linked blocks. Used in cryptocurrency, supply chain, and secure records. Appeared in Gr12 theory papers. Know: what it is, how it works, and 2 uses.
AI-generated fake videos or audio that appear authentic. Social implications question topic — impacts on trust, truth, and politics. Know the definition and two risks.
A part of the internet not indexed by search engines, accessed via special software (Tor). Used for anonymity — legal and illegal uses. Appeared in theory cybercrime questions.
The principle that all internet traffic should be treated equally — ISPs cannot throttle or prioritise certain content. A social/political IT topic.
The successor to IPv4 — uses 128-bit addresses (vs 32-bit) to solve address exhaustion. Format: 2001:0db8:85a3::8a2e:0370:7334. Know why it was introduced.
RAID 0: speed (striping, no redundancy). RAID 1: mirroring (exact copy). RAID 5: striping with parity (balance of speed + fault tolerance). CAPS only mentions RAID in general — exams go deeper.

Hiding secret information within ordinary files (images, audio) without detection. Different from encryption — encryption scrambles data; steganography hides its existence. Appeared in theory questions.
Self-executing contracts where terms are written in code on a blockchain. No middlemen needed. Related to blockchain technology — appeared in Gr12 social implications.
Processing data close to where it is generated (the "edge" of the network) rather than sending it all to a central cloud server. Reduces latency — important for IoT and 5G.

All devices share a single communication line (the bus). Not in the current CAPS but appeared in older papers. Advantage: cheap. Disadvantage: single point of failure; performance drops with more devices.
Manipulating people (rather than systems) to reveal confidential information. Includes pretexting, baiting, tailgating. Appears in cybercrime theory questions.
Exams frequently ask you to
convert
values — not just explain systems. Practise: decimal↔binary, decimal↔hex, binary↔hex. Know the place values (powers of 2 and 16).
South African law governing how personal data must be collected, stored, and protected. Appears in privacy and legal implications questions. Know: what it is, who it protects, and 2 requirements.
SA legislation that criminalises hacking, ransomware, online fraud, and malicious communications. Often referenced in cybercrime theory questions for Gr12.
A virtual replica of a physical object or system, updated in real time from sensor data. Used in engineering, manufacturing, and smart cities. Appeared in 4IR/IoT questions.

runs.t: A comprehensive reference of all Delphi functions you need to know — both CAPS-required and commonly appearing in exams.

rows.t: Position of substring (0 if not found)
Insert text into string at position
base to the power of exp
String → Integer (safe — returns default if invalid)
Off-by-one errors in loops; forgetting to initialise variables
Using wrong index (0 vs 1-based); not closing file
Class with constructor, accessors, mutators, toString (~25–30 marks)
Calling Create on the object var (not class name); forgetting Result in functions
TADOQuery, SQL statements including JOIN (~20–25 marks)
Not calling qry.Close before changing SQL; wrong JOIN ON condition
Write a recursive function; trace it (~10–15 marks)
Missing base case; not returning Result
Correct normal form identification; proper primary/foreign key use
Balance of positive AND negative effects; specific examples
Correct crime type names; effects on both victim and business

runs.t: Read the question twice — "list" means bullet points, "explain" means a sentence or two
Never leave a practical question blank — partial marks are awarded for correct structure
In SQL: always check your WHERE condition matches the question's criteria exactly
In OOP: write the constructor first — it sets up all the attributes you'll use elsewhere
In theory: "advantage AND disadvantage" means you need both for full marks
